BWhat is Next.js?+

A React framework by Vercel that adds: file-based routing, SSR, SSG, ISR, Server Actions, API routes, middleware, image/font optimization, and built-in Tailwind/CSS support. It's the recommended way to build production React apps in 2026. Current version: 16.2.

BWhat is the App Router?+

Next.js routing system using the app/ directory. File conventions: page.tsx = route UI, layout.tsx = shared wrapper, loading.tsx = Suspense fallback, error.tsx = error boundary, not-found.tsx = 404. Routes are defined by folder structure: app/about/page.tsx → /about.

BWhat is the Pages Router?+

Legacy routing using pages/ directory. All components are Client Components. Data fetching via getServerSideProps/getStaticProps. No Server Components. Still supported but App Router is recommended for new projects. Migration guide available.

BWhat is a layout in Next.js?+

Shared UI that wraps child pages. Defined in layout.tsx. Key feature: layouts DON'T re-render on navigation — state persists (sidebar stays open). Root layout is required (wraps entire app with html/body). Nested layouts are composable: app/dashboard/layout.tsx wraps all dashboard pages.

BWhat is the difference between Server and Client Components?+

Server (default): runs on server, zero JS to client, can access DB directly, can't use useState/useEffect/onClick. Client: marked with 'use client', includes interactivity, JS sent to browser. Default to Server; opt into Client only for interactivity.

BWhat is a dynamic route?+

Route with variable segments: app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx → /blog/any-slug. Access params: const { slug } = await params. Catch-all: [...slug] matches /a/b/c. Optional catch-all: [[...slug]] also matches /.

BWhat is the loading.tsx file?+

Automatically creates a Suspense boundary around the page. Shows while the page is loading (Server Component fetching data). No manual Suspense needed. Renders instantly from cache on navigation while the new content loads. Creates a smooth loading experience.

BWhat is the error.tsx file?+

Automatically creates a React error boundary. Catches errors in the page and child components. Receives error and reset props. Must be a Client Component ('use client'). Isolates errors — rest of the app continues working. global-error.tsx catches root layout errors.

BWhat is the not-found.tsx file?+

Shown when notFound() is called or a route doesn't exist. Customize per route segment. The root app/not-found.tsx handles all unmatched URLs (404 page).

BWhat is the Link component?+

import Link from 'next/link'. Client-side navigation without full page reload. Prefetches linked pages in the background (viewport/hover). Replaces <a> tags for internal navigation. Supports href, replace (no history entry), scroll (control scroll position), prefetch (true/false).

BWhat is the Image component?+

import Image from 'next/image'. Auto-optimizes: converts to WebP/AVIF, lazy loads, prevents layout shift (CLS), responsive sizing. Props: src, width, height, alt (required), priority (above fold), placeholder='blur', sizes (responsive). Much better than plain <img>.

BWhat is the Metadata API?+

SEO metadata in App Router. Static: export const metadata = { title: '...', description: '...' }. Dynamic: export async function generateMetadata({ params }) { ... }. Supports: title template, OpenGraph, Twitter cards, robots, sitemap, icons. Replaces react-helmet entirely.

BWhat is a route handler (API route)?+

app/api/users/route.ts — server-side endpoint. Export named functions: GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE. Receives Request object, returns Response. Runs on server only. Use for: external API consumption, webhooks, third-party integrations.

BWhat is a Server Action?+

Async function marked with 'use server' that runs on the server. Used for data mutations (form submissions, DB writes). Can be called from forms: <form action={createUser}>. No API route needed. Supports progressive enhancement (works without JS). The modern way to handle mutations in Next.js.

BWhat is the difference between template.tsx and layout.tsx?+

layout.tsx: persists across navigation, state maintained, component instance reused. template.tsx: re-mounts on every navigation, fresh state. Use layout for most cases. Use template when you need: route transition animations, useEffect on every navigation, or reset state per page.

BWhat is next/font?+

Automatic font optimization. import { Inter } from 'next/font/google'. Self-hosts fonts (no external requests), eliminates layout shift with size-adjust, supports variable fonts. Generates CSS variables for use in Tailwind. Zero CLS font loading out of the box.

BHow do you add global CSS in Next.js?+

Import in root layout: import './globals.css'. Supports: global CSS, CSS Modules (*.module.css), Tailwind CSS (built-in), Sass (npm install sass). CSS Modules are scoped to component. Tailwind is the most popular choice in 2026.

BWhat is the next.config.js file?+

Configuration file at project root. Controls: redirects, rewrites, headers, image domains, environment variables, experimental features, Turbopack settings, output mode (standalone, export). Use next.config.ts for TypeScript support (Next.js 15+).

BWhat is static export in Next.js?+

output: 'export' in next.config — generates pure static HTML/CSS/JS (no server needed). Deployed to any static host (GitHub Pages, S3, Netlify). Limitations: no SSR, no API routes, no middleware, no ISR. Good for: documentation sites, landing pages, portfolios.

BWhat is the useRouter hook?+

import { useRouter } from 'next/navigation' (App Router). Methods: router.push('/path') (navigate), router.replace('/path') (no history), router.back(), router.forward(), router.refresh() (refetch server data). Must be in a Client Component.

IExplain SSG vs SSR vs ISR vs CSR.+

SSG: HTML at build time, served from CDN. Fastest. For static content. SSR: HTML per request. For dynamic/personalized content. ISR: Static + revalidation — pages regenerate in background after a time interval. Best of both. CSR: Client-side only, via 'use client' + useEffect. Use CSR only when Server Components won't work (heavy client interaction, third-party widgets).

IWhat is Partial Prerendering (PPR)?+

Next.js 16's most powerful feature. Renders static parts at build time (served from CDN instantly) while dynamic parts stream via Suspense. Example: product page where header/grid are static (instant) but cart/recommendations are dynamic (streamed). Eliminates the old static-vs-dynamic tradeoff. Uses Suspense boundaries to define the split.

IWhat is middleware in Next.js?+

Code that runs BEFORE request completes. middleware.ts at project root. Runs on Edge Runtime. Can: redirect, rewrite URLs, set headers, check auth, handle geolocation, A/B testing. Use config.matcher to specify routes. Cannot: access databases directly (Edge limitations). Use for request-level logic, not heavy computation.

IExplain data fetching in Server Components.+

Server Components can fetch data directly — no useEffect, no client-side loading states. async function Page() { const data = await db.query(...); return <List data={data}/> }. Parallel: const [a,b] = await Promise.all([...]);. Sequential: when one fetch depends on another. Data fetching happens on the server, reducing client bundle and waterfall requests.

IWhat is revalidation? (revalidatePath vs revalidateTag)+

revalidatePath('/blog'): clears cache for a specific path. revalidateTag('posts'): clears all entries tagged with 'posts'. Use in Server Actions after mutations. Time-based: fetch(url, { next: { revalidate: 3600 } }) — stale after 1 hour. On-demand is more precise; time-based is simpler. Both trigger background regeneration.

IWhat are route groups?+

Folders wrapped in parentheses: (marketing), (shop). Don't affect URL. Use to: organize routes logically, apply different layouts to different sections without changing URLs. app/(marketing)/about/page.tsx → /about (not /marketing/about). Useful for separating auth layout from main layout.

IWhat are parallel routes?+

Named slots with @folder convention. Layout receives them as props: function Layout({children, modal, sidebar}). Each slot renders independently. Use for: modals that work with URL, dashboards with independent sections, split-view UIs. Requires default.tsx for unmatched slots.

IWhat are intercepting routes?+

Show a route as a modal/overlay without full navigation. Convention: (..)photo/[id] intercepts /photo/:id one level up. On click: shows photo in modal. On direct URL/refresh: shows full page. Used for: photo galleries (Instagram-style), preview modals, quick views. Combines with parallel routes for the modal pattern.

IHow does authentication work in Next.js?+

Three layers: 1) Middleware: check session cookie, redirect unauthenticated users (fast, Edge). 2) Server Components: const user = await getUser(), redirect if not logged in. 3) Client: Context/Zustand for client auth state. Libraries: Auth.js (NextAuth), Clerk, Lucia. Server-first approach keeps sensitive logic off client.

IWhat is the 'use cache' directive?+

Next.js 16: ALL code is dynamic by default. Cache explicitly with 'use cache' on pages, components, or functions. Compiler auto-generates cache keys. Control lifetime: cacheLife('hours'). Replaces old implicit caching (Next.js 15 cached fetch by default — confusing). Much more predictable.

IWhat is generateStaticParams?+

Pre-generates static pages for dynamic routes at build time. export async function generateStaticParams() { const posts = await getPosts(); return posts.map(p => ({ slug: p.slug })); }. Combined with the page component, this creates static HTML for each slug. The equivalent of getStaticPaths from Pages Router.

IWhat is streaming in Next.js?+

Sends HTML in chunks as components render, instead of waiting for everything. Enabled automatically with loading.tsx and Suspense boundaries. User sees content progressively. Non-blocking: fast components render immediately, slow ones stream in later. Works with SSR and RSC. Dramatically improves Time to First Byte and First Contentful Paint.

IWhat is the redirect() function?+

Server-side redirect: import { redirect } from 'next/navigation'; redirect('/login'). Works in Server Components and Server Actions. Throws internally (must not be in try/catch). For conditional redirects: check auth, then redirect. Client-side: use useRouter().push(). For permanent redirects: permanentRedirect() (308 status).

IHow does error handling work in Next.js?+

error.tsx: catches rendering errors per route segment, shows fallback UI with reset button. global-error.tsx: catches root layout errors (replaces entire HTML). not-found.tsx: handles notFound() calls. Server Actions: return error objects, display in UI via useActionState. API routes: return Response with error status. Error boundaries recover gracefully without full page crash.

IWhat is the Adapter API (Next.js 16)?+

Stable interface allowing any hosting provider to deploy Next.js fully. Previously, advanced features (ISR, middleware) worked best on Vercel. Now Cloudflare, AWS, Netlify can implement the adapter to support all features. Addresses 'vendor lock-in' criticism. OpenNext is a community adapter for AWS. Makes Next.js truly platform-agnostic.

IWhat is Turbopack?+

Rust-based bundler replacing webpack. Default in Next.js 16. 5-10x faster Fast Refresh during dev, 2-5x faster production builds. File system caching: compile artifacts stored on disk for faster restarts. Server Fast Refresh (16.2): fine-grained server-side hot reloading. 200+ bug fixes in 16.2. The performance difference is immediately noticeable.

IWhat is the Next.js MCP server?+

Model Context Protocol integration for AI-assisted development. Next.js DevTools MCP exposes tools for AI agents: get_routes (list all routes), debugging info, diagnostics. 16.2 adds AGENTS.md in create-next-app, browser log forwarding to terminal for AI debugging. Treats AI agents as first-class users of the framework.

IHow do you handle environment variables in Next.js?+

.env.local for local dev, .env.production for prod. Server-only by default. Prefix with NEXT_PUBLIC_ to expose to client (embedded in JS bundle). Access: process.env.DATABASE_URL (server), process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL (client). Never put secrets in NEXT_PUBLIC_ variables.

IWhat is the instrumentation.ts file?+

Runs once when server starts. Use for: initializing monitoring (OpenTelemetry, Sentry), setting up database connections, registering custom metrics. export async function register() { /* setup code */ }. Runs in both Node.js and Edge runtimes. The right place for one-time server initialization.

IWhat is sitemap.ts and robots.ts?+

Generate SEO files dynamically. app/sitemap.ts: export default function sitemap(): MetadataRoute.Sitemap { return [{url:'...', lastModified:new Date()}] }. app/robots.ts: export default function robots(): MetadataRoute.Robots { return {rules:{allow:'/'}} }. Auto-generated at build/request time.

AExplain the rendering lifecycle of a Next.js request.+

1) Request hits server. 2) Middleware runs (if matched). 3) Route is matched to page. 4) Layout hierarchy is resolved. 5) Server Components render (can fetch data, access DB). 6) RSC payload is generated. 7) HTML is streamed to client (with Suspense boundaries). 8) Client receives HTML, shows content immediately. 9) React hydrates Client Components. 10) Page is fully interactive.

AHow does Next.js caching work internally? (all 4 layers)+

1) Request Memoization: deduplicates identical fetch calls in same render pass. 2) Data Cache: persists fetch results across requests/deploys ('use cache' or revalidate options). 3) Full Route Cache: caches rendered HTML and RSC payload for static routes. 4) Router Cache: client-side cache of visited routes for instant back/forward. Each layer has its own invalidation mechanism. Understanding these layers is key to debugging cache issues.

AWhat is incremental adoption from Pages to App Router?+

Both routers can coexist. Strategy: 1) Start with new pages in App Router. 2) Migrate shared layouts first. 3) Move pages one at a time. 4) Replace getServerSideProps with Server Components. 5) Replace getStaticProps with generateStaticParams + 'use cache'. 6) Replace API routes with Server Actions where possible. 7) Remove Pages Router when done. The next/link and next/image work in both.

AHow do you optimize Core Web Vitals in Next.js?+

LCP: Use priority on hero images, SSR/SSG for fast HTML, font optimization. FID/INP: Minimize client JS, use Server Components, code-split with lazy. CLS: next/image prevents shift, next/font eliminates FOIT, proper Suspense fallbacks with matching dimensions. Tools: Lighthouse, web-vitals library, Vercel Speed Insights. Next.js defaults handle most optimizations automatically.

AExplain Server Component serialization and the RSC payload.+

When a Server Component renders, its output is serialized into an RSC payload — a special JSON-like format that describes the component tree. This payload is streamed to the client where React deserializes it and renders the actual DOM. The payload includes: rendered HTML strings, references to Client Component bundles, and serialized props passed from Server to Client Components. Props must be serializable (no functions, classes, or DOM nodes).

AWhat is the CVE-2025-55182 (React2Shell)?+

Critical vulnerability (CVSS 10.0) in React Server Component deserialization allowing unauthenticated remote code execution. Discovered Nov 2025, fixed Dec 3. Next.js 15.x and 16.x affected. Exploitation observed within hours of disclosure by state-nexus threat groups. Lesson: SSR introduces server-side attack surfaces — keep dependencies updated, pin versions, monitor advisories.

AHow does next/image optimization work under the hood?+

Images are optimized on-demand (first request) and cached. Pipeline: 1) Request with width/quality params. 2) Server resizes/compresses image. 3) Converts to modern format (WebP/AVIF based on Accept header). 4) Caches result with TTL. 5) Serves cached version for subsequent requests. Supports: remote images (configure domains), blur placeholder (base64 data URL), responsive srcset generation. Eliminates manual image optimization workflows.

AExplain the proxy.ts feature in Next.js 16.+

Replaces middleware for network proxy use cases. Runs before the request reaches your application code. Clarifies the network boundary — middleware was overloaded with both request modification and proxying concerns. proxy.ts handles URL rewriting, request forwarding, and header manipulation at the network level. Middleware still exists for Edge-specific logic (auth checks, A/B testing).

AHow do you implement real-time features in Next.js?+

1) Server-Sent Events: Stream from route handler with ReadableStream. 2) WebSockets: Separate WS server (Socket.io, ws) — Next.js doesn't handle WebSocket upgrades. 3) Polling: Client-side with TanStack Query's refetchInterval. 4) Third-party: Pusher, Ably, Supabase Realtime. For most apps, polling with TanStack Query or SSE is sufficient. WebSockets add deployment complexity.

AWhat is the difference between Edge and Node.js runtime?+

Edge Runtime: lightweight, globally distributed, cold starts <1ms, limited APIs (no fs, no native modules, some Node.js APIs missing). Use for: middleware, simple API routes, auth checks. Node.js Runtime (default): full Node.js APIs, database drivers, any npm package. Use for: data-heavy operations, database queries, file processing. Set per route: export const runtime = 'edge'.

AHow do you handle internationalization (i18n) in Next.js?+

1) next-intl/next-i18next: Popular libraries with App Router support. 2) Route-based: app/[locale]/page.tsx with middleware for locale detection. 3) Middleware: detect Accept-Language header, redirect to appropriate locale. 4) Server Components: pass locale to components, fetch translations on server. 5) Metadata: use generateMetadata with locale param for localized SEO. Key: keep translations on server to reduce client bundle.

AWhat is the stable_action_id and how do Server Actions work internally?+

Each Server Action gets a unique ID at build time. When the client submits a form, it sends a POST request to the current URL with the action ID in the body. The server matches the ID to the corresponding function and executes it. This is why Server Actions work without API routes — they're embedded in the page's server-side handler. The action ID ensures the right function runs even with multiple actions on one page.

AHow do you deploy Next.js to non-Vercel platforms?+

Docker: output: 'standalone' in config, creates self-contained build. AWS: OpenNext adapter, deploy to Lambda/CloudFront. Cloudflare: @cloudflare/next-on-pages adapter. Netlify: Built-in Next.js adapter. Static: output: 'export' for any static host. The Adapter API (Next.js 16) standardizes this — any provider can implement the interface for full feature support.

Angular

88 questions — basic to very advanced. Click any question to reveal the answer.

Showing 88 questions
BeginnerWhat is Angular? How is it different from AngularJS?+

Angular (2+) is a complete TypeScript-based rewrite. AngularJS used JavaScript, controllers, and $scope. Angular uses components, TypeScript, hierarchical DI, and is vastly faster due to AOT compilation. They share nothing except the name.

BeginnerWhat is a component?+

A reusable UI building block combining a TypeScript class (logic), an HTML template (view), and CSS (styles), linked by the @Component() decorator.

BeginnerWhat are the four types of data binding?+

Interpolation {{ }}: display data. Property binding [ ]: set element properties. Event binding ( ): react to actions. Two-way binding [( )]: read and write simultaneously.

[square brackets] = data goes IN. (parentheses) = data comes OUT. [(banana in a box)] = both directions.
BeginnerWhat is the difference between ngOnInit and the constructor?+

The constructor is a TypeScript/JS feature. Use it only for DI. ngOnInit is an Angular lifecycle hook called after inputs are set. Use it for API calls, subscriptions, and setup logic.

Constructor = you're born (only get your name). ngOnInit = first day of school (now you can start learning).
BeginnerWhat are directives? Name the three types.+

Component directives — components are directives with templates. Structural directives — add/remove DOM elements (*ngIf, *ngFor). Attribute directives — change appearance/behavior (ngClass, ngStyle).

BeginnerWhat are pipes? Give examples.+

Pipes transform displayed data in templates without changing the underlying data. Built-in: date, currency, uppercase, lowercase, percent, json, async, slice, keyvalue, titlecase.

BeginnerWhat is a service in Angular?+

A class decorated with @Injectable() that encapsulates reusable logic — API calls, auth, caching, logging. Typically singleton when provided in root. Services keep components lean.

BeginnerWhat is dependency injection?+

A design pattern where classes receive their dependencies from the outside instead of creating them. Angular's DI system automatically creates, manages, and provides service instances.

Without DI: you cook every meal yourself. With DI: you call room service and it brings exactly what you need.
BeginnerWhat is the Angular CLI?+

A command-line tool (ng) that scaffolds projects, generates components/services, serves dev builds, runs tests, and builds for production. It enforces best practices and consistent structure.

BeginnerWhat is the purpose of NgModule?+

NgModule was the original way to group related components, directives, and pipes. It declares what belongs together, what to import, and what to export. Since Angular 14, standalone components replace the need for modules in most cases.

BeginnerWhat is interpolation?+

Double curly braces {{ expression }} that evaluate a TypeScript expression and render the result as text in the DOM. Supports property access, ternary operators, pipe calls, and method calls (though methods should be avoided for performance).

BeginnerWhat is *ngIf and how does @if differ?+

*ngIf is a structural directive that conditionally adds/removes an element from the DOM. @if (Angular 17+) is the new built-in control flow syntax — same function, but it's built into the template compiler (better performance, type narrowing, no extra import needed).

BeginnerWhat is a template reference variable?+

Declared with # syntax: <input #nameInput>. It gives you a reference to the DOM element or component instance, usable within the template: (click)="greet(nameInput.value)".

BeginnerWhat is the async pipe?+

| async subscribes to an Observable or Promise in the template and automatically unsubscribes when the component is destroyed. It also triggers change detection on new values. It's the preferred way to handle async data.

BeginnerWhat is a standalone component?+

A component with standalone: true that declares its own imports directly in the decorator, without needing an NgModule. This is the default and recommended approach since Angular 14.

BeginnerWhat is the difference between [class] and [ngClass]?+

[class.active]="isActive" toggles a single class. [ngClass] accepts strings, arrays, or objects to manage multiple classes. For one class, use [class.x]. For complex logic, use [ngClass].

IntermediateExplain change detection in Angular.+

Zone.js monitors all async operations. When any complete, Angular runs change detection from the root component downward, comparing binding values. If different, it updates the DOM. OnPush strategy limits checks to input reference changes, internal events, async pipe emissions, or manual triggers.

Default = a paranoid guard checking every room every 5 minutes. OnPush = a smart alarm that only alerts when a specific door opens.
IntermediateWhat is OnPush change detection? When should you use it?+

OnPush tells Angular to skip checking this component unless: an @Input reference changes, an event fires inside it, an Observable emits via async pipe, a signal changes, or markForCheck() is called. Use it on every component — it's the single biggest performance optimisation.

IntermediateTemplate-driven vs reactive forms — when use which?+

Template-driven: Logic in HTML via ngModel. Good for simple forms (login, search). Reactive: Logic in TypeScript via FormGroup/FormControl. Good for complex forms (multi-step, dynamic fields, custom validation). Reactive forms are easier to test because logic is in TypeScript.

IntermediateExplain switchMap vs mergeMap vs concatMap vs exhaustMap.+

switchMap: cancels previous, uses latest. (Search typeahead) mergeMap: runs all in parallel. (File uploads) concatMap: sequential queue. (Ordered saves) exhaustMap: ignores new until current finishes. (Login button — prevents double-submit)

switchMap = "Cancel my burger, I want pizza." mergeMap = "Cook burger AND pizza simultaneously." concatMap = "Finish the burger, then start pizza." exhaustMap = "I'm still eating, ignore new orders."
IntermediateWhat are route guards? List all types.+

canActivate — can user enter this route? canDeactivate — can user leave? (unsaved changes) canActivateChild — protect all children. canMatch — should this route definition even be considered? resolve — pre-fetch data before activation.

IntermediateWhat is lazy loading and why does it matter?+

Lazy loading splits your app into chunks loaded on demand. Instead of downloading the entire app upfront, users only get the code for the current page. Implemented with loadComponent or loadChildren. Dramatically reduces initial load time.

Like Netflix: you stream the episode you're watching, not the entire catalog.
IntermediateWhat is content projection (ng-content)?+

Allows parent components to inject HTML into child component slots. Single-slot uses <ng-content />. Multi-slot uses select attribute to target named slots. Essential for reusable wrappers like cards, modals, and tabs.

IntermediateWhat are interceptors? Give real examples.+

HTTP middleware that intercepts every request/response. Real uses: attach JWT tokens, global error handling (catch 401 → redirect to login), loading spinners, response caching, request retry logic, logging.

Like airport security: every passenger (request) passes through, gets checked, and potentially modified — without individual gates knowing.
IntermediateWhat is ViewEncapsulation?+

Emulated (default): Scopes CSS via attribute selectors — styles don't leak out, but global styles can leak in. ShadowDom: Native Shadow DOM — complete isolation. None: No encapsulation — styles become global.

IntermediateExplain the difference between Subject, BehaviorSubject, and ReplaySubject.+

Subject: No initial value. Late subscribers miss past emissions. BehaviorSubject: Has current value. New subscribers get the latest immediately. ReplaySubject: Buffers N past values. New subscribers get the replay. AsyncSubject: Only emits the last value on completion.

IntermediateWhat is trackBy (track) and why does it matter?+

When Angular re-renders a list, it needs to know which items changed. Without tracking, it destroys and recreates all DOM elements. With track item.id, Angular reuses existing elements and only updates changed ones. Huge performance win for lists.

IntermediateHow does Angular handle security (XSS, CSRF)?+

XSS: Angular sanitizes all template bindings by default, stripping dangerous content from innerHTML, style, and URL bindings. CSRF: HttpClient auto-reads XSRF-TOKEN cookie and sends it as X-XSRF-TOKEN header.

IntermediateWhat is ng-container and when do you use it?+

ng-container is a grouping element that doesn't get rendered to the DOM. Use it when you need a structural directive but don't want an extra wrapper element. Common uses: wrapping multiple elements with *ngIf, combining *ngIf and *ngFor on the same content.

IntermediateWhat is the purpose of environment files?+

Environment files store configuration that varies between builds: API URLs, feature flags, analytics keys. Angular's build system swaps them at compile time. Use fileReplacements in angular.json to map environment.ts to environment.prod.ts.

IntermediatePure vs impure pipes — what's the difference?+

Pure (default): Only re-runs when the input reference changes. Efficient, works with immutable data. Impure: Re-runs on every change detection cycle. Needed to detect mutations inside arrays/objects, but very expensive. Prefer pure pipes + immutable updates.

IntermediateHow do you share data between unrelated components?+

1. Shared service with BehaviorSubject or signal — components inject the same service and read/write to the shared state. 2. NgRx/SignalStore for complex state. 3. Route params/query params for URL-driven state. The service approach covers 90% of cases.

IntermediateWhat are resolvers?+

Resolvers pre-fetch data before a route activates. The component receives the data immediately, avoiding loading states. Implemented as functions returning Observables, Promises, or plain values.

IntermediateWhat is AOT vs JIT compilation?+

AOT (Ahead-of-Time): Templates compiled during build. Smaller bundles, faster rendering, catches template errors at build time. Used in production. JIT (Just-in-Time): Templates compiled in the browser at runtime. Larger bundles, slower startup. Only used during development (and rarely now).

IntermediateWhat is the difference between @ViewChild and @ContentChild?+

@ViewChild queries elements in the component's own template. @ContentChild queries elements projected via ng-content from the parent. ViewChild is available in ngAfterViewInit; ContentChild in ngAfterContentInit.

IntermediateHow do you create a custom structural directive?+

Inject TemplateRef and ViewContainerRef. Based on your condition, call viewContainer.createEmbeddedView(templateRef) to show or viewContainer.clear() to hide. Use an @Input setter to react to condition changes.

IntermediateWhat is pathMatch: 'full' vs 'prefix'?+

prefix (default): Matches if the URL starts with the path. full: Matches only if the entire URL matches. Critical for redirects: { path: '', redirectTo: '/home', pathMatch: 'full' } — without 'full', every URL would match the empty string prefix.

IntermediateWhat is the host property in @Component?+

The host property lets you bind classes, attributes, styles, and events directly to the component's host element (the custom HTML tag itself), without needing a wrapper <div> in the template.

IntermediateWhat is forRoot() vs forChild() pattern?+

A convention for configuring NgModules. forRoot() provides services as singletons in the root module. forChild() provides only declarations, no singleton services. Prevents duplicate service instances in lazy-loaded modules. With standalone, provideRouter() and provideHttpClient() replace this pattern.

AdvancedExplain Angular's hierarchical DI system in depth.+

Two parallel trees: Environment injector tree (Root → Platform → lazy route injectors) and Element injector tree (mirrors component tree). Resolution walks up the element tree first, then the environment tree. Modifiers: @Self, @SkipSelf, @Optional, @Host control where to look. Providers can use useClass, useValue, useFactory, useExisting.

AdvancedWhat are Angular Signals and how do they change the framework?+

Signals are synchronous reactive primitives: signal() (writable), computed() (derived), effect() (side effects). Unlike RxJS, they always have a current value and don't need subscriptions. They enable fine-grained reactivity where only components reading a changed signal update. Long-term, they'll replace Zone.js entirely.

AdvancedExplain Zone.js and what happens without it.+

Zone.js monkey-patches all async APIs (setTimeout, Promise, addEventListener, XHR, fetch). It creates execution contexts that track async tasks, allowing Angular to know when to run change detection. Without it (zoneless mode), you rely on signals and explicit notifications. Benefits: ~15KB smaller bundle, cleaner stack traces, no monkey-patching quirks.

AdvancedHow does SSR and hydration work in Angular?+

Angular Universal renders on the server → sends HTML to browser → fast FCP + SEO. Hydration (v16+): Instead of destroying server DOM and rebuilding, Angular "attaches" to existing nodes. Incremental hydration (v19+): Combines @defer with SSR — server renders HTML, but JS for deferred blocks loads only when triggered.

AdvancedHow would you optimize a large Angular application?+

1. OnPush everywhere. 2. Lazy load routes. 3. @defer for heavy components. 4. track by ID in @for. 5. Virtual scrolling for long lists. 6. Preload lazy chunks. 7. Signals over BehaviorSubject. 8. SSR with hydration. 9. Bundle analysis. 10. Web Workers for heavy computation. 11. Avoid function calls in templates. 12. Use CDK's scrolling and drag-drop modules.

AdvancedWhat is the inject() function vs constructor injection?+

inject() works in field initializers, constructors, and factory functions. Advantages: enables composable utility functions, works in functional guards/interceptors, cleaner syntax. Limitation: can only be called during construction (injection context), not in methods called later.

AdvancedWhat is the difference between markForCheck() and detectChanges()?+

markForCheck(): Marks this component and all ancestors as dirty. Change detection will check them on the next cycle. Respects the normal top-down CD flow. detectChanges(): Immediately triggers CD for this component and its children, right now. Use sparingly — it breaks the normal flow and can cause issues.

AdvancedExplain InjectionToken and when you'd use it.+

InjectionToken provides DI for non-class values (strings, numbers, objects, functions). Use cases: API URLs, configuration objects, feature flags, abstract interfaces. Tokens can include a factory function for tree-shakable defaults.

AdvancedHow does Angular animation work under the hood?+

Angular animations use the Web Animations API under the hood. The @angular/animations module provides a declarative DSL: trigger defines an animation name, state defines named states with styles, transition defines how to animate between states, animate specifies duration/easing/delay. Complex features include query (animate children), stagger (delayed sequential animations), and group (parallel animations).

AdvancedExplain deferrable views (@defer) and their impact on performance.+

@defer enables component-level code splitting in templates. The deferred component's code is split into a separate chunk and loaded when triggered (viewport, idle, interaction, hover, timer, or boolean condition). Supports @placeholder, @loading (with minimum duration to prevent flicker), and @error blocks. Replaces complex lazy loading patterns for many use cases.

AdvancedWhat is the resource() API?+

resource() is a signal-based API for async data fetching. It takes a request function (returns params derived from signals) and a loader function (fetches data). It auto-refetches when request signal dependencies change and provides .value(), .isLoading(), .error(), and .status() signals. rxResource() is the Observable-based variant.

AdvancedWhat is linkedSignal?+

linkedSignal (Angular 19+) creates a writable signal that auto-resets when its source signal changes, but can also be set manually. Use case: a selected tab that resets to the first tab when the tab list changes, but the user can still click to select a different one.

AdvancedHow would you handle complex state management?+

Level 1 — Service + Signals: For most apps. Simple, testable. Level 2 — NgRx SignalStore: Structured store with features. Level 3 — NgRx Store (Redux): Actions, reducers, effects, selectors. Maximum predictability for very large apps. Don't reach for NgRx until services become hard to reason about.

AdvancedWhat are Angular schematics?+

Schematics are code generators/transformers used by the CLI. They describe changes to a file tree (create, modify, delete files). Libraries use ng add schematics for auto-setup, and ng update schematics for migrations. You can create custom schematics for your team's patterns.

AdvancedExplain how Angular handles i18n.+

Angular's built-in i18n uses i18n attribute on elements. At build time, Angular extracts marked text into XLIFF/XMB files for translation. It builds a separate bundle per locale — each is a fully compiled app. Supports pluralization and ICU expressions. Alternative: runtime libraries like ngx-translate or Transloco for single-bundle translations.

AdvancedWhat design patterns do you use in Angular architecture?+

Smart/Dumb — Separate data from presentation. Facade — Simplify complex service interactions. Repository — Abstract data access for testability. Adapter — Transform API data at the boundary. Strategy — Swap implementations via InjectionTokens. Observer — RxJS Observables/Subjects. Mediator — Central service coordinating sibling components.

AdvancedWhat is a multi-provider and when would you use it?+

Setting multi: true on a provider lets multiple providers for the same token coexist. Instead of overriding, Angular collects all into an array. Use cases: multiple HTTP interceptors, multiple validators, plugin systems where multiple implementations register themselves.

AdvancedHow do you implement a custom form control (ControlValueAccessor)?+

Implement ControlValueAccessor interface with 4 methods: writeValue() — receive value from form. registerOnChange() — store callback to push changes. registerOnTouched() — store callback for blur/touched. setDisabledState() — handle disabled. Register as NG_VALUE_ACCESSOR provider. This makes custom components work with ngModel and formControl.

AdvancedExplain ExpressionChangedAfterItHasBeenChecked error.+

Angular runs change detection twice in dev mode (the second is a verification pass). If a binding's value changes between the two runs, you get this error. Common causes: modifying state in ngAfterViewInit, modifying parent state from child lifecycle hooks. Fixes: use setTimeout, move logic to ngOnInit, or use signals which batch updates correctly.

AdvancedWhat is withComponentInputBinding() and why is it useful?+

A router feature that automatically binds route params, query params, and resolved data to matching component @Input() properties. Instead of injecting ActivatedRoute and extracting params manually, the router does it for you. Cleaner, less boilerplate, works with signal inputs too.

AdvancedWhat is the Renderer2 and when would you use it?+

Renderer2 is an abstraction over DOM manipulation. Instead of directly accessing nativeElement, use Renderer2 methods (addClass, setStyle, setAttribute, listen). Benefits: platform-agnostic (works in SSR, Web Workers), testable, and secure (avoids direct DOM access that bypasses sanitization).

AdvancedWhat are Angular elements (custom elements)?+

Angular Elements lets you package Angular components as standard Web Components (Custom Elements). They can be used in any HTML page, even without Angular. Use createCustomElement() from @angular/elements. Great for: micro-frontends, embeddable widgets, gradual migration from other frameworks.

IntermediateWhat is the DestroyRef and takeUntilDestroyed?+

DestroyRef (Angular 16+) provides a way to register cleanup callbacks. takeUntilDestroyed() is a pipe operator that automatically completes an Observable when the component/service is destroyed. It's the cleanest way to handle unsubscription, replacing the Subject + takeUntil pattern.

IntermediateWhat is the difference between providedIn: 'root' and providing in a component?+

providedIn: 'root': One singleton instance for the entire app. Tree-shakable if unused. providers: [Service] in a component: Creates a new instance for each instance of that component and its descendants. Use component-level for services that should NOT be shared globally.

IntermediateHow do you handle errors in HTTP requests?+

Component level: catchError operator in the pipe chain. Global level: Error interceptor that catches specific status codes (401 → login redirect, 500 → error page). Retry: retry({ count: 3, delay: 1000 }) for transient failures. Always transform errors into user-friendly messages.

BeginnerWhat is *ngFor and how does @for differ?+

*ngFor is a structural directive for iterating arrays. @for (Angular 17+) is the new built-in control flow — same purpose but compiled by Angular's template compiler for better performance. @for requires track and supports @empty blocks.

BeginnerWhat is two-way data binding and how does it work?+

Two-way binding combines property binding and event binding. [(ngModel)]="name" is shorthand for [ngModel]="name" (ngModelChange)="name = $event". It syncs the model and the view — when either changes, the other updates.

IntermediateWhat is ngTemplateOutlet?+

ngTemplateOutlet instantiates an ng-template with an optional context object. It lets you render templates dynamically and pass data via context. Use cases: customisable table rows, configurable list item templates, dynamic form layouts.

IntermediateWhat is the async/await equivalent in Angular (with Observables)?+

Use firstValueFrom() or lastValueFrom() to convert Observables to Promises: const user = await firstValueFrom(this.http.get('/api/user')). However, the RxJS pipe approach is usually better because it supports cancellation, retry, and composition.

AdvancedWhat is a dynamic component and how do you create one?+

Use ViewContainerRef.createComponent() to instantiate components programmatically at runtime. Pass a component class reference. Set inputs via componentRef.setInput(). Use cases: modals, dynamic form fields, plugin architectures. With @defer, many dynamic component use cases are now simpler.

AdvancedWhat is tree-shaking and how does Angular support it?+

Tree-shaking removes unused code from the final bundle. Angular supports it through: providedIn: 'root' (services only included if imported), standalone components (only bundled when imported), and AOT compilation (dead code analysis at build time). The build tool (esbuild/webpack) performs the actual tree-shaking.

AdvancedHow do you implement a global error handler?+

Provide a custom ErrorHandler: { provide: ErrorHandler, useClass: GlobalErrorHandler }. The handleError(error) method catches all unhandled errors. Use it for: logging to external services (Sentry, DataDog), showing user-friendly error toasts, tracking error metrics. Combine with HTTP error interceptor for API errors.

IntermediateWhat is the router-outlet?+

<router-outlet> is a placeholder directive where the router renders matched components. You can have multiple named outlets for auxiliary routes: <router-outlet name="sidebar">. The component rendered inside changes based on the current URL.

IntermediateWhat is the difference between forwardRef and regular references?+

forwardRef() lets you reference a class that hasn't been defined yet (declared later in the file). TypeScript/JavaScript normally requires classes to be defined before they're referenced. Common use: circular dependencies, NG_VALUE_ACCESSOR providers referencing the component class being defined.

AdvancedExplain the difference between effect() and computed() in signals.+

computed() derives a new value from other signals — it's pure, has no side effects, and is lazy (only recalculates when read). effect() runs side effects when signals change — logging, API calls, DOM manipulation. Key rule: never write to signals inside computed(), but you can inside effect() (though it's discouraged).

AdvancedWhat is signal-based change detection (zoneless)?+

In zoneless Angular, there's no Zone.js. Change detection is driven entirely by signals. When a signal changes, Angular schedules an update only for components that read that signal. This is true fine-grained reactivity — no more checking the entire tree. Enable with provideExperimentalZonelessChangeDetection().

IntermediateWhat is the CDK (Component Dev Kit)?+

The CDK provides behavior primitives without opinionated styling: virtual scrolling, drag-and-drop, overlays (dialogs, tooltips), accessibility (focus trap, live announcer), clipboard, layout (breakpoint observer). It's the foundation Angular Material is built on, but you can use it with any design system.

AdvancedHow would you implement micro-frontends with Angular?+

Options: 1. Module Federation (webpack) — share dependencies at runtime between separately deployed Angular apps. 2. Angular Elements — wrap components as Web Components, embed in any shell app. 3. Native Federation — framework-agnostic alternative using import maps. 4. Single-SPA — orchestrate multiple framework apps. Challenges: shared state, routing coordination, dependency versioning.

AdvancedWhat are Signal Forms in Angular 21? How do they differ from Reactive Forms?+

Signal Forms (experimental in v21) replace RxJS-based form state with signals. Instead of new FormGroup(), you use a form() function that wraps a signal model. Form values, validity, dirty/touched state are all signals — read them directly with .value(), .valid(). No more valueChanges.subscribe() or takeUntilDestroyed(). They combine the simplicity of template-driven forms with the power of reactive forms while being fully type-safe.

Reactive Forms = you subscribe to a newspaper and wait for delivery. Signal Forms = the newspaper is always on your desk, automatically updated — you just look at it whenever you want.
IntermediateWhat does "zoneless Angular" mean and why is it the default in v21?+

Zoneless means Angular no longer depends on Zone.js to detect changes. Instead, change detection is driven entirely by signals. When a signal changes, only components reading that signal update. Benefits: ~15KB smaller bundle, faster INP (Interaction to Next Paint), native async/await without issues, cleaner stack traces. Angular 21 makes this the default for new projects because all signal primitives became stable in v20.

IntermediateHow do you migrate an existing Angular app from Zone.js to zoneless?+

1. Run ng generate @angular/core:zoneless schematic. 2. Ensure all components use OnPush change detection. 3. Replace manual state mutations with signals. 4. Replace .subscribe() in components with async pipe or signal-based patterns. 5. Replace provideZoneChangeDetection() with provideZonelessChangeDetection(). 6. Remove zone.js from polyfills. 7. Test thoroughly — any component relying on Zone.js auto-detection will need manual updates.

IntermediateWhat is Vitest and why did Angular 21 adopt it over Karma?+

Vitest is a Vite-native test runner that's significantly faster than Karma/Jasmine. Angular 21 makes it the default because: it runs zoneless by default (matching the new runtime), supports modern features like fake timers, snapshots, and parallelisation, uses the intuitive vi mocking API, and doesn't require a browser to run. Migrate existing projects with ng generate @angular/core:karma-to-vitest.

AdvancedWhat is the Angular MCP Server?+

The MCP (Model Context Protocol) Server bridges your Angular codebase with AI models like Gemini or Claude. Launched via ng mcp, it provides tools: find_examples (curated code examples), get_best_practices (Angular best practices), ai_tutor (interactive learning), search_documentation (official docs search), onpush_zoneless_migration (migration help), and modernize (legacy code modernisation). It enables context-aware AI code generation that understands YOUR project structure.

IntermediateWhat is @angular/aria?+

A new library (developer preview in v21) providing unstyled, accessible UI directives implementing WAI-ARIA patterns. Includes tree views, tabs, accordions, listboxes, dialogs — each with keyboard navigation, ARIA attributes, focus management, and screen reader support built in. Designed as foundations for custom component libraries, not for direct app use. Important for EU Accessibility Act compliance.

BeginnerWhat is Hot Module Replacement (HMR) in Angular?+

HMR updates your app in the browser instantly when you save a file, without a full page reload. Angular 19 introduced CSS and template HMR, and Angular 20 graduated template HMR to stable. This means editing a template or style file reflects immediately — no losing component state or waiting for recompilation.

AdvancedWhat are selectorless components (expected in Angular 22)?+

Currently, components require a string selector: 'app-user-card'. Selectorless components would remove this requirement — you'd import the component class directly in templates. Benefits: better refactorability (rename the class, all references update), type safety (no string matching), and simpler mental model. This aligns with the standalone-first architecture where components are self-describing.

AdvancedWhat Angular CVEs were disclosed in late 2025 and what did they affect?+

Three major CVEs: CVE-2025-59052 (CVSS 7.1) — SSR race condition leaking request-scoped data between concurrent requests. CVE-2025-66035 (CVSS 7.7) — HttpClient leaking XSRF tokens via protocol-relative URLs. CVE-2025-66412 (CVSS High) — Stored XSS in template compiler via SVG/MathML attributes. All patched in v19.2+, v20, v21. This demonstrates why running EOL versions is a serious security risk.

IntermediateWhat is the evolution of signals across Angular versions?+

v16: Signals introduced (developer preview). v17: Signal inputs, model inputs, view queries added. v18: linkedSignal, resource() added. v19: Signal primitives polished. v20: ALL signal primitives graduated to stable (signal, computed, effect, linkedSignal, signal queries, signal inputs). v21: Signal Forms (experimental), zoneless-by-default (signals drive CD). v22: Signal Forms expected to stabilise, OnPush expected as default.

AdvancedHow does Angular 21 handle testing in a zoneless world?+

With Vitest as default, tests run zoneless — matching the runtime. Signal-based state is synchronous, so assertions work without fakeAsync or tick() for signal updates. For async operations, Vitest's fake timers replace Angular's zone-based testing utilities. fixture.detectChanges() still works but is needed less often since signal-based components update more predictably. The key shift: test signal values directly rather than querying DOM.
